message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 9 (36 todo)
Recent Green phase: No code change — "7 runes → 175" was a confirming test already satisfied by the exact-block rule.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. No implementation changed this cycle. Evaluate naming and Four Rules; compute APP mass/McCabe. If nothing is warranted (likely), document why.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by measuring the current state of the code before deciding on any refactoring.
Now let me run the measurement pipeline. First, ESLint and the test suite.

content.text:
PRE measurements established:
- **ESLint smells**: 0 (clean — `messages: []`)
- **Tests**: 9 passing, 36 todo

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

| Function | Purpose (current tests) | Name fits? |
|---|---|---|
| `componentGroupBase` | base value for a group of N components (block rule) | Yes — reveals the per-group block calc |
| `isComponent` | is this item a rune/moonstone? | Yes |
| `mainItemBase` | sum base premium of non-component items | Yes |
| `componentBase` | sum base of components, grouped by type with block rule | Yes |
| `roundUpToWhole` | ceil after fixing float precision | Yes |
| `quotePremium` | full premium for one quote step | Yes |
| `runScenario` | map steps → premiums | Yes |

All names reveal intent.

**Step 2 & 3: APP Mass + McCabe per function**

| Function | APP Mass (breakdown) | McCabe |
|---|---|---|
| `componentGroupBase` | 1 binding + 1 conditional(4) + 1 const + 2 invoc(`===`,`*`)→ ~1+4+1+4=10 | 2 (one ternary) |
| `isComponent` | 1 binding + 1 invoc(2) = 3 | 1 |
| `mainItemBase` | 1 binding + 3 invoc(filter,reduce,+)+ 1 invoc(isComponent) + 1 const ≈ 1+8+1 = ~10 | 1 |
| `componentBase` | assignment(6)+loop(5)+conditional(4 for `??`)+invocs ≈ 24 | 3 (for-loop, filter, `??` branch) |
| `roundUpToWhole` | 1 binding + 2 invoc(ceil,toFixed) = 5 | 1 |
| `quotePremium` | 1 binding + several invocs ≈ 11 | 1 |
| `runScenario` | 1 binding + map invoc ≈ 7 | 1 |

**Worst offender**: `componentBase` — McCabe 3, mass ~24. It is the only function with imperative mutation (the `counts` accumulator + `++` via `??`).

Let me evaluate a refactoring of `componentBase`: the manual loop-and-mutate grouping could become a declarative group-and-sum, removing the assignment (mass 6) and the loop mutation.

Considering the alternative:
```ts
const componentBase = (items: Item[]): number => {
  const counts = items.filter(isComponent).reduce<Record<string, number>>(
    (acc, item) => ({ ...acc, [item.type]: (acc[item.type] ?? 0) + 1 }), {});
  return Object.values(counts).reduce((sum, count) => sum + componentGroupBase(count), 0);
};
```

This trades the explicit `for` loop + assignment for a `reduce` with spread. Let me weigh it: the spread-in-reduce removes one assignment (mass −6) and the loop (mass −5) but adds reduce + spread invocations and is widely considered *less* clear (object-spread-in-reduce is a known readability smell, and O(n²)). McCabe stays at 3 (the `??` branch and two reduces remain). Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP mass here — the current explicit loop reads more clearly and is more efficient. This change would be a net loss.

Let me verify there are no genuinely simpler wins by checking the lint config for thresholds, to confirm nothing is near a limit.

input.description: Inspect ESLint config and project root

content.text:
The config confirms the thresholds. The worst function `componentBase`:
- cognitive-complexity threshold is 10 — `componentBase` is well under (≈3)
- max-depth 3 — the function has one `for` with one statement, depth 1
- max-lines-per-function 30 — it is 7 lines
- no-magic-numbers — all numbers extracted to named constants
- All other smells: clean (ESLint reported zero)

**Decision: No refactoring performed.**

I evaluated the one candidate (declarative grouping for `componentBase`) and rejected it: it would not reduce McCabe (stays 3), would marginally lower APP mass by trading a clear `for` loop for an object-spread-in-`reduce` that is harder to read (violates Rule 2, which trumps APP) and is O(n²). Reverting before applying is the correct call — no change beats a clarity-negative change.

Per the rules, I must document all four PRE measurements to justify "no refactoring":

```
Refactoring Evaluation (worst offender: componentBase)
- ESLint smells:        0  (file fully clean)
- Cognitive complexity: ~3 (threshold 10)
- APP mass:             ~24 (the only function with mutation; minimal for the grouping it expresses)
- McCabe cyclomatic:    3  (for-loop + filter + ?? branch — irreducible for grouped counting)
- Naming:               all 7 functions reveal intent

Reasoning: The implementation is already well-factored — constants are
named, functions are small and single-purpose, and no smell fires. The
only structural candidate (spread-in-reduce grouping) lowers mass slightly
but worsens clarity and performance with no McCabe gain. Rule 2 trumps APP.
```

Verifying tests remain green (no code was changed, so they are):

content.text:
Refactoring cycle complete. No implementation changed this cycle (the Green phase was a confirming test), and measurement confirms no refactoring is warranted.

```
Refactoring Complete

Refactoring:            none performed (already clean — clarity-negative trade rejected)
Smell delta:            0 → 0 (ESLint reports zero violations)
Cognitive delta:        ~3 → ~3 (componentBase; threshold is 10)
APP mass delta:         ~24 → ~24 (componentBase, the worst offender)
McCabe delta:           3 → 3 (componentBase — irreducible for grouped counting)
Tests:                  9/9 passing, 36 todo
```

**Why no refactoring:** The file is fully clean against all configured smells (cognitive-complexity, max-depth, max-lines-per-function, no-magic-numbers, duplication, etc.). I identified `componentBase` as the worst offender (McCabe 3, the only mutating function) and evaluated converting its explicit `for`-accumulate loop into a declarative `reduce`-with-spread grouping. That change was rejected before applying: it leaves McCabe at 3, only marginally lowers APP mass, and trades a clear, O(n) loop for a harder-to-read, O(n²) spread-in-reduce. Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP mass, so the current code wins. All 7 function names reveal intent; no rename needed.
